task: Implement a thread-safe LRU cache in Rust with TTL expiry, bounded memory, and lock-free reads using Arc, RwLock, and background eviction, including comprehensive error handling

criteria.description: Evaluates correct memory ordering semantics in atomic operations, proper barrier placement, and understanding of concurrency model limitations. Expert-level work demonstrates understanding that Arc<RwLock<T>> cannot achieve true lock-free reads and either acknowledges this limitation or implements genuine lock-free alternatives.
criteria.pass_condition: Uses appropriate memory ordering (acquire-release for state transitions, relaxed for counters) with documented rationale for each choice, implements proper synchronization around cache state modifications, and either correctly identifies that RwLock reads aren't lock-free OR implements true lock-free reads with hazard pointers/epochs

sub_attributes.description: Correct memory ordering on atomic operations affecting cache consistency
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if uses acquire-release ordering consistently with documented reasoning for each choice, 0.7 if uses acquire-release but inconsistently, 0.4 if uses relaxed ordering with some barriers, 0.0 if uses only relaxed or no atomic operations

sub_attributes.description: Honest assessment of whether lock-free reads are actually achieved
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if implements true lock-free reads with atomics/hazard pointers OR explicitly documents that RwLock reads aren't lock-free, 0.7 if attempts lock-free with minor correctness issues, 0.4 if claims lock-free but uses RwLock without acknowledgment, 0.0 if incorrectly claims RwLock provides lock-free reads

sub_attributes.description: Prevention of ABA problems in pointer-based operations
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if uses epoch-based reclamation or hazard pointers for memory management, 0.7 if uses generation counters or versioning, 0.4 if acknowledges ABA risk but incomplete mitigation, 0.0 if no ABA consideration in lock-free components

pass_examples: Uses AtomicPtr<CacheNode> with epoch-based reclamation for lock-free reads
Explicitly documents 'RwLock reads require shared lock acquisition, not truly lock-free' with performance implications
fail_examples: Claims Arc<RwLock<HashMap>> provides lock-free reads without qualification
Uses relaxed ordering on cache size counters that affect eviction decisions
criteria.research_basis: Herlihy & Shavit's 'The Art of Multiprocessor Programming' memory model requirements and Rust's atomic ordering documentation specifying acquire-release semantics for cross-thread visibility

criteria.description: Evaluates lock ordering discipline and deadlock prevention strategies in cache operations with background threads.
criteria.pass_condition: Establishes consistent lock ordering hierarchy with documented acquisition order, implements timeout mechanisms for lock acquisition, and either documents deadlock prevention strategy or demonstrates systematic lock ordering discipline
pass_examples: Documents lock hierarchy: 'Always acquire cache_map before eviction_queue before metrics_counter' with try_lock timeouts
Background eviction thread uses try_write_for(Duration) to prevent indefinite blocking
fail_examples: Background thread holds write lock while waiting for channel notification
Eviction callback acquires cache lock while already holding TTL timer lock
criteria.research_basis: Coffman conditions for deadlock occurrence and industry best practices from high-throughput caching systems like Redis and Memcached's lock hierarchy designs

criteria.description: Evaluates the correctness and efficiency of LRU ordering maintenance under concurrent access, including proper handling of promotion/demotion operations and resistance to race conditions.
criteria.pass_condition: Implements O(1) access/update operations using intrusive doubly-linked list or equivalent with atomic pointer updates, provides atomic promotion/demotion operations with proper synchronization, and demonstrates understanding of ordering invariant preservation under concurrent access

sub_attributes.description: Time complexity analysis of LRU operations (get, put, promote) under concurrent access
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if all operations proven O(1) amortized with detailed complexity analysis, 0.7 if O(1) average case with some O(log n) worst-case scenarios, 0.4 if O(n) operations present but acknowledged, 0.0 if O(n²) or worse

sub_attributes.description: Maintenance of strict LRU ordering invariants during concurrent modifications
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if invariants proven with formal verification or exhaustive testing showing 100% correctness, 0.7 if stress testing shows ≥99% correctness with identified edge cases, 0.4 if basic testing with some ordering violations under load, 0.0 if frequent ordering corruption

sub_attributes.description: Selection and implementation of optimal data structure for concurrent LRU operations
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if uses intrusive doubly-linked list with HashMap or lock-free skip list variant, 0.7 if uses standard library structures with custom ordering logic, 0.4 if uses suboptimal structures like Vec with linear search, 0.0 if uses fundamentally incorrect approach

pass_examples: Intrusive doubly-linked list with atomic next/prev pointers and CAS operations for reordering, proven O(1) through mathematical analysis
fail_examples: Uses Mutex-protected Vec with linear search for LRU victim selection
Doubly-linked list implementation with race conditions allowing nodes to be orphaned during concurrent promote operations
criteria.research_basis: Based on Herlihy & Shavit's 'The Art of Multiprocessor Programming' analysis of lock-free data structures

criteria.description: Evaluates TTL timestamp consistency, background eviction thread lifecycle, and timer precision under concurrent access using lock-free timer wheels or careful synchronization between cache operations and background cleanup.
criteria.pass_condition: TTL operations maintain consistency under concurrent modification using either lock-free timestamp updates or careful write-lock coordination, background thread uses proper shutdown signaling with ≤100ms cleanup latency, and timer wheel or similar O(1) expiration structure

sub_attributes.description: Atomic updates to TTL timestamps preventing race conditions
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if uses AtomicU64 timestamps with compare-and-swap updates, 0.7 if uses write locks consistently for TTL updates, 0.4 if uses read locks but potential races, 0.0 if unprotected TTL timestamp updates

sub_attributes.description: Proper spawning, shutdown, and resource cleanup of eviction thread
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if implements graceful shutdown with JoinHandle and bounded cleanup time, 0.7 if uses shutdown signal but unbounded wait, 0.4 if background thread but manual cleanup required, 0.0 if background thread without proper lifecycle

sub_attributes.description: O(1) or O(log n) expiration checking rather than linear scans
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if implements timer wheel or heap-based expiration queue, 0.7 if uses BTreeMap for time-ordered expiration, 0.4 if sorted vector with binary search, 0.0 if linear scan for expired entries

pass_examples: AtomicU64::compare_exchange_weak for TTL updates with retry loop
tokio::time::interval with graceful shutdown using CancellationToken
fail_examples: Background thread sets TTL using cache.write().entry().ttl = new_time without atomic coordination
Uses std::thread::sleep for periodic cleanup without shutdown mechanism
criteria.research_basis: Timer wheel algorithm from Varghese & Lauck's 'Hashed and Hierarchical Timing Wheels' and Tokio's cancellation token patterns for async runtime integration

criteria.description: Evaluates memory usage bounds enforcement, reference counting correctness, and memory leak prevention strategies.
criteria.pass_condition: Implements memory tracking and eviction when approaching limits, uses proper Arc/Weak patterns to prevent reference cycles, and provides mechanisms for bounded memory growth with clear eviction policies

sub_attributes.description: Strict adherence to configured memory limits under concurrent load
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if memory usage never exceeds bounds + 5% buffer during stress testing, 0.7 if occasionally exceeds by up to 15%, 0.4 if frequent exceedance but eventual compliance, 0.0 if unbounded memory growth

sub_attributes.description: Proper Arc/Weak usage to prevent reference cycles in cache node structures
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if uses Weak references for back-pointers and parent relationships with documented cycle analysis, 0.7 if mostly correct with minor potential cycles, 0.4 if some Arc cycles but manual cleanup, 0.0 if Arc cycles causing memory leaks

sub_attributes.description: Memory leak prevention in long-running cache instances
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if demonstrates stable memory usage over 24+ hours with <1MB/hour growth, 0.7 if stable over shorter periods with acceptable growth rate, 0.4 if some leaks but bounded, 0.0 if unbounded memory leaks

pass_examples: Cache entries use Weak<CacheNode> for LRU list back-pointers to prevent cycles
Memory usage tracked with AtomicUsize and enforced before insertions
fail_examples: Cache nodes with Arc references to parent creating reference cycles
Memory bounds checked only during eviction, allowing temporary unbounded growth
criteria.research_basis: Rust ownership patterns from the Rust Book and memory leak analysis patterns from production cache implementations

criteria.description: Evaluates adherence to Rust API guidelines, proper ownership patterns, lifetime management, and zero-cost abstractions with idiomatic Rust patterns
criteria.pass_condition: API follows Rust naming conventions for 100% of public interfaces with comprehensive documentation examples, implements all appropriate standard traits with custom derives where applicable, demonstrates zero-allocation hot paths through flamegraph analysis or equivalent profiling, and uses advanced type system features (phantom types, associated types, const generics) to enforce invariants at compile time
pass_examples: Implements Debug, Clone for cache configuration structs
Uses AsRef<str> for key parameters enabling both String and &str usage
fail_examples: Public methods use camelCase instead of snake_case naming
Forces unnecessary String clones for &str keys in get operations
criteria.research_basis: Official Rust API Guidelines and patterns from std library implementation analysis

criteria.description: Evaluates async runtime integration choices, proper resource management in async contexts, and consistency with stated concurrency model.
criteria.pass_condition: If implementing async patterns: uses tokio::sync primitives consistently, implements proper cancellation handling with cleanup guarantees, and spawns background tasks with graceful shutdown. If using std::sync: documents rationale for sync-only design and provides clear interface boundaries

sub_attributes.description: Proper use of async-aware synchronization primitives
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if uses tokio::sync::RwLock and other async primitives correctly, 0.7 if mixes async and sync primitives appropriately, 0.4 if some async primitives but potential blocking, 0.0 if uses std sync primitives in async context

sub_attributes.description: Proper handling of task cancellation and resource cleanup
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if implements cancellation tokens with guaranteed cleanup and no resource leaks, 0.7 if handles cancellation but some potential resource issues, 0.4 if basic cancellation handling, 0.0 if no cancellation safety considerations

sub_attributes.description: Integration with standard async ecosystem patterns and libraries
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if follows tokio patterns and integrates with async ecosystem standards, 0.7 if good integration with minor deviations, 0.4 if basic async support but limited ecosystem integration, 0.0 if poor async ecosystem integration

pass_examples: Uses tokio::sync::RwLock with .await for non-blocking async operations
Background tasks use CancellationToken for graceful shutdown with resource cleanup
fail_examples: Uses std::sync::RwLock in async functions causing potential blocking
Background async tasks without cancellation handling leading to resource leaks
criteria.research_basis: Tokio documentation on async synchronization patterns and async Rust ecosystem best practices

criteria.description: Evaluates whether the concurrency model properly supports the cache algorithms and TTL expiry system without introducing race conditions between LRU ordering updates, TTL expiration checks, and memory bound enforcement
criteria.pass_condition: Demonstrates that LRU promotion/demotion operations are atomic with respect to TTL expiration checks, memory bound enforcement doesn't interfere with cache ordering invariants, and background eviction maintains consistency across all cache subsystems

sub_attributes.description: Atomic coordination between LRU operations and TTL expiration
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if LRU promotions and TTL updates occur atomically preventing expired entries from being promoted, 0.7 if mostly coordinated with rare race conditions, 0.4 if some coordination but potential inconsistencies, 0.0 if LRU and TTL systems operate independently with race conditions

sub_attributes.description: Memory bound enforcement integration with cache ordering systems
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if eviction due to memory pressure respects LRU ordering and doesn't corrupt cache structure, 0.7 if mostly consistent with minor ordering issues, 0.4 if some integration but potential corruption, 0.0 if memory bounds and cache ordering conflict

sub_attributes.description: Maintenance of invariants across all cache subsystems during concurrent operations
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if formal analysis or comprehensive testing shows all subsystems maintain consistency, 0.7 if extensive testing with high confidence in cross-system consistency, 0.4 if basic cross-system testing, 0.0 if subsystems can interfere with each other's invariants

pass_examples: LRU promotion operation checks TTL validity atomically within same critical section
Memory eviction uses LRU ordering and updates TTL expiration queue consistently
fail_examples: Expired entries can be promoted to front of LRU queue due to race between TTL check and promotion
Memory pressure eviction removes entries without updating LRU ordering structures
criteria.research_basis: Concurrency control theory from database systems and consistency models for distributed cache systems

criteria.description: Evaluates whether error handling strategies are consistent across all subsystems (concurrency, TTL, memory management, async runtime) and whether error recovery maintains cache-wide consistency rather than leaving subsystems in inconsistent states
criteria.pass_condition: Error handling maintains consistency across LRU ordering, TTL expiration, and memory bounds simultaneously, async task failures don't corrupt cache state, and recovery procedures restore all subsystems to consistent state within defined time bounds

sub_attributes.description: Error handling maintains consistency across all cache subsystems
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if errors in any subsystem trigger coordinated cleanup across all related subsystems, 0.7 if mostly coordinated with some potential inconsistencies, 0.4 if basic error handling but potential cross-system corruption, 0.0 if subsystem errors can corrupt other subsystems

sub_attributes.description: Async task failures don't compromise cache integrity
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if async task panics or cancellations maintain cache invariants with proper cleanup, 0.7 if mostly isolated with minor potential issues, 0.4 if some isolation but potential cache corruption, 0.0 if async failures can corrupt cache state

sub_attributes.description: Error recovery procedures address all subsystems simultaneously
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if recovery procedures restore all subsystems to consistent state with defined time bounds, 0.7 if comprehensive recovery with some time uncertainty, 0.4 if basic recovery procedures, 0.0 if no coordinated recovery across subsystems

pass_examples: TTL background thread failure triggers coordinated cleanup of LRU ordering and memory tracking with cache entering degraded mode
Memory allocation failures during LRU promotion maintain both LRU invariants and TTL consistency
fail_examples: Memory exhaustion during LRU reordering leaves cache in inconsistent state with broken ordering
Background TTL thread panic leaves expired entries in LRU queue without cleanup mechanism
criteria.research_basis: Fault tolerance principles from distributed systems and transaction processing consistency models

criteria.description: Evaluates performance optimization strategies while ensuring thread safety guarantees and error handling capabilities are maintained, with clear documentation of design tradeoffs
criteria.pass_condition: Implements strategies to reduce lock contention and uses cache-friendly data structures while maintaining thread safety properties through formal analysis or extensive testing, ensures lock-free optimizations don't compromise error recovery capabilities, and provides quantitative analysis of safety/performance tradeoffs with documented rationale

sub_attributes.description: Thread safety guarantees maintained despite performance optimizations
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if performance optimizations proven to maintain safety through formal analysis or exhaustive testing, 0.7 if extensive testing shows safety preservation, 0.4 if basic verification but some uncertainty, 0.0 if optimizations compromise safety guarantees

sub_attributes.description: Error recovery capabilities preserved in optimized code paths
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if optimized paths maintain full error recovery capabilities with no degradation, 0.7 if mostly preserved with minor limitations, 0.4 if some error handling limitations in optimized paths, 0.0 if optimizations compromise error recovery

sub_attributes.description: Clear documentation and quantitative analysis of design tradeoffs
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if provides quantitative analysis of safety/performance/maintainability tradeoffs with benchmarks and reasoning, 0.7 if good documentation with some quantitative support, 0.4 if basic tradeoff discussion, 0.0 if no tradeoff analysis or documentation

pass_examples: Lock-free read optimizations include formal proof that ABA prevention doesn't interfere with error cleanup procedures
Documents 'chose RwLock over lock-free for 10% performance cost but 90% error handling simplicity' with benchmarks
fail_examples: Lock-free optimizations that disable error recovery in fast paths
Performance optimizations with no analysis of safety implications or error handling impact
criteria.research_basis: Software engineering principles for safety-critical systems and performance engineering methodology from systems programming literature
